answer:
Being a good friend, to me, means showing up when it counts and keeping the small promises that build trust. The two big things I try to live by are loyalty and honesty—mixed with a dash of humor so things never feel heavy.

Loyalty shows up in the everyday stuff. Last semester my roommate got sick right before mid-terms. I brewed endless pots of tea, ran to the pharmacy at midnight, and traded my own study notes so she could rest. It wasn’t dramatic, but it told her, “I’ve got your back.”  

Honesty is the other pillar. When my buddy Jake posted a photo that accidentally revealed another friend’s private info, I texted him right away: “Hey, take that down—Sarah won’t like it.” He grumbled at first, but later he thanked me for saving him from an awkward apology tour.

I also keep a running “inside-joke” streak—sending goofy memes the second I know someone’s having a rough day. It’s my way of saying, “I see you, and I’m here.” Put all that together, and I guess my recipe for friendship is simple: be present, be real, and always bring the meme when the mood dips.
title: Good friends are important in life. What do you think it means to be a good friend? Which characteristics do you have that make you a good friend? Include specific details and examples from your life in your response.
